title: The Fading Promise of Higher Education
gist: Hedgehog Review editor Jay Tolson surveys a Summer 2026 thematic issue on the collapsing public trust in American higher education (Gallup confidence down from 57% to 36% since 2015, up to $8.2B in Trump-administration grant cuts, AI-driven classroom degradation, and a shrinking humanities job market), framing Clark Kerr's 1960s 'multiversity' concept and Yale's own Committee on Trust reforms as the throughline, alongside a companion symposium on literacy and myth-making around the 250th anniversary of 1776.

insights.claim: Clark Kerr's 1963 book The Uses of the University argued higher education had outgrown the old ideal types of the 'academic cloister of Cardinal Newman' and the 'research organism of Abraham Flexner,' with American universities now also running investment funds and hospitals, fielding minor-league sports teams, and providing job training companies no longer offer.
insights.speaker: Jay Tolson
insights.so_what: Explains why today's university crisis is multidimensional (finance, healthcare, athletics, workforce training, not just teaching/research) — any single-lever fix (e.g. funding reform alone) will be incomplete.
insights.quote: the “academic cloister of Cardinal Newman” and the “research organism of Abraham Flexner”
insights.locator: In his 1963 book, The Uses of the University

insights.claim: Clark Kerr, who coined the term 'multiversity,' was ousted from the University of California in 1967 by Governor Ronald Reagan in retaliation for the Free Speech Movement protests at Berkeley — the first in a recurring pattern of university presidents (recently at Harvard, Columbia, and the University of Virginia) becoming casualties of the culture war.
insights.speaker: Jay Tolson
insights.so_what: A 60-year precedent that politically-triggered university-president ousters are cyclical, not a new phenomenon — useful for calibrating how much the recent wave of resignations (Harvard, Columbia, UVA) actually signals structural change.
insights.quote: casualties of the culture war over American universities.
insights.locator: Ousted from his perch at the University of California in 1967

insights.claim: A Yale committee report found public confidence in higher education fell from 57% in 2015 to 36% in 2024, driven by three forces: the high and rising cost of attendance, concerns over the admissions process, and criticism of what is taught on campus, including free speech, political bias, and self-censorship.
insights.speaker: Jay Tolson
insights.so_what: A three-factor scorecard (cost, admissions, viewpoint climate) for tracking whether any given university's reform package actually addresses the drivers of public distrust versus just its symptoms.
insights.quote: what is said and taught on university campuses, including matters of free speech, political bias, and self-censorship.
insights.locator: A widely discussed report released in April

insights.claim: The Trump administration has terminated as much as $8.2 billion in grants to universities and withheld funds from Harvard, UCLA, Columbia, and Duke, charging them with promoting antisemitism, indoctrinating students, and rigging admissions along racial lines.
insights.speaker: Jay Tolson
insights.so_what: Quantifies the direct federal-funding risk now facing named elite universities — an $8.2B figure that is the proximate financial catalyst behind the institutional-trust crisis.
insights.quote: terminated as much as $8.2 billion in grants to universities and withheld funds from universities such as Harvard, UCLA, Columbia, and Duke
insights.locator: The Trump administration, motivated by critics

insights.claim: Universities have capitulated to political pressure faster than expected — shuttering diversity programs, scrubbing gender-related language from their websites, and adopting an overnight neutral communications tone after a decade of activism-inflected administrator emails — a shift that has surprised and dismayed liberals while cheering conservatives.
insights.speaker: Jay Tolson
insights.so_what: Signals that elite universities are more financially and politically vulnerable to federal pressure than their public activism suggested — relevant to how durable any given university's DEI/political posture actually is under funding threats.
insights.quote: shuttering diversity programs, scrubbing mentions of gender from their online materials, and adopting, overnight, a strenuously neutral tone
insights.locator: The speed with which many storied institutions have capitulated

insights.claim: A decline in international student enrollment, partly a result of Trump-administration policy, is compounding demographic declines in the domestic college-age population, already forcing school closures (Hampshire College in Massachusetts) and cuts to humanities majors that don't bring in money.
insights.speaker: Jay Tolson
insights.so_what: Identifies a demographic/enrollment headwind independent of the political funding fight — relevant to which institutions (especially smaller, humanities-heavy colleges) face closure risk regardless of how the federal-funding dispute resolves.
insights.quote: forcing many institutions to close their doors (as Hampshire College, in Massachusetts, recently announced it would)
insights.locator: A decrease in the number of international students

insights.claim: AI chatbots are degrading the campus pedagogical environment by making it easy for students to shirk reading and instructors to shirk grading; per Dennis M. Hogan's essay, administrators are adopting the technology despite faculty pushback on cost-cutting grounds, though the real drivers of rising tuition may be less honorable than claimed.
insights.speaker: Jay Tolson
insights.so_what: A specific, named mechanism (AI adoption for administrative cost-cutting) worth tracking as both a driver of further academic-quality erosion and a potential vector for further tuition increases, not decreases.
insights.quote: degrading the pedagogical environment on college campuses, making it easy for students to shirk reading and instructors to shirk grading.

insights.claim: Public universities' shift to a student-loan-reliant funding model created a market that rewards schools for recruiting deep-pocketed out-of-state students over in-state residents and channels poorer students into less desirable majors, turning former engines of egalitarianism into contributors to inequality, according to Marshall Steinbaum and David Austin Walsh.
insights.speaker: Jay Tolson
insights.so_what: Identifies a structural funding-model driver of inequality baked into public-university admissions economics — relevant for assessing whether state-school reform proposals actually change recruitment incentives.
insights.quote: created a market that rewarded schools for selling out their own young people in favor of deep-pocketed students from out of state
insights.locator: How America's Public Universities Stopped Being Public

insights.claim: Tenure-track humanities jobs have declined so sharply that Mary Townsend describes a generation of scholars as feeling 'as though they are taking religious vows just as the monasteries are closing.'
insights.speaker: Jay Tolson
insights.so_what: A vivid indicator of labor-market collapse in academic humanities — relevant to assessing long-run supply/demand and wage pressure for PhD-holding humanities labor.
insights.quote: We now have a generation of scholars who feel as though they are taking religious vows just as the monasteries are closing.
insights.locator: Who Wants to Be a Professor?

insights.claim: Benjamin S. Bernard's companion essay frames the current crisis as the latest in countless historical breakdowns of university-society relations tied to technological, cultural, and political shifts, and suggests embracing greater 'epistemic humility' could again allow universities to renegotiate their standing, as calibrated concessions have in the past.
insights.speaker: Jay Tolson
insights.so_what: Positions 'epistemic humility' (institutional self-criticism and reform) as the historically successful playbook for universities recovering public trust after past crises — a benchmark for judging whether current reform gestures are substantive.
insights.quote: Might embracing greater “epistemic humility” allow them to do so again now?
insights.locator: university-society relations have broken down countless times

insights.claim: The Yale Committee on Trust's first recommendation was for universities to accept their share of responsibility for declining public confidence, though Tolson questions whether 'the hour is too late for mea culpas.'
insights.speaker: Jay Tolson
entities: Yale Committee on Trust in Higher Education
insights.so_what: Raises real doubt about whether institutional self-criticism can still repair public trust given how far confidence has already fallen (57% to 36%) — a skepticism worth weighing against optimistic reform narratives.
insights.quote: is the hour too late for mea culpas?
insights.locator: The first recommendation of the Yale report

themes.theme: The 1776 symposium: literacy and myth-making in the founding era

insights.claim: Nicholas Carr's essay argues Benjamin Franklin's overhaul of the colonial postal system was more than pragmatic infrastructure — it was, in Carr's words, 'an attempt to turn America itself into a republic of letters,' reflecting Enlightenment ideals of communal, free-flowing thought.
insights.speaker: Jay Tolson
insights.so_what: Frames early American media infrastructure (the postal system) as a deliberate nation-building project, a historical analogy for how today's information infrastructure (AI, social platforms) shapes national cohesion.
insights.quote: It was an attempt to turn America itself into a republic of letters.

insights.claim: Brooke Allen's essay credits Thomas Paine's Common Sense (1776) with making him 'perhaps the greatest 'influencer' in our nation's history,' framing Paine's pamphleteering career as an early precursor to modern mass-persuasion media.
insights.speaker: Jay Tolson
insights.so_what: Draws a direct lineage between Founding-era pamphleteering and today's influencer/attention economy — a useful historical anchor for assessing how novel current information-warfare dynamics really are.
insights.quote: Common Sense (1776) made him perhaps the greatest ‘influencer’ in our nation’s history.
insights.locator: To Begin the World Over Again

insights.claim: Martha Bayles praises Ken Burns's PBS documentary on the American Revolution for its 'evenhandedness' in depicting the era's slavery and conquest of Native Americans without letting those evils overshadow the Revolution's achievements, or vice versa.
insights.speaker: Jay Tolson
insights.so_what: Signals which historical treatments of contested national narratives are being held up as models of balance — relevant context for media/cultural-content producers navigating politically charged historical subject matter.
insights.quote: such evenhandedness is, to say the least, refreshing.
